books.title: The Alarm That Cries Wolf
books.subtitle: For the founder whose body sounds the danger bell before every launch, pitch, and send — learning to tell a real threat from a false alarm
books.desc: Your heart is pounding, your hands are cold, and nothing has actually happened yet. This is for the founder whose alarm system fires the moment something matters — bracing for a danger that, this time, isn't there. You'll learn to feel the difference between a real threat and a false alarm, and to take the one step that's yours to take anyway.
books.long_desc:
The email is written. The pitch deck is finished. The button says Publish, and your whole body has just decided this is a five-alarm emergency.

Your pulse climbs. Your stomach drops. Some old part of you scans the room for the exit, certain that something terrible is about to happen — even though, if you're honest, nothing has. This is the false alarm: the oldest, fastest part of you firing as if the launch were a predator, as if a hard conversation could end your life. It cannot tell the difference between mortal danger and a thing that simply matters very much.

This book is for the founder who braces. The one who has learned to read a quickened heartbeat as proof they shouldn't do the thing — who has called off, delayed, and watered down more brave moves than they can count, because the body rang the bell and they believed it. You are not a coward. You are running a magnificent, ancient warning system that keeps mistaking your courage for a threat.

Chapter by chapter, you'll come to recognize the false alarm by name. You'll feel where it lives — the cold hands, the shallow breath, the urge to flee a danger that exists only as a forecast. You'll learn the actual difference between a body protecting you from harm and a body protecting you from growth. And you'll meet the cliff-edge question that runs through this whole series: what is one thing you can do before you feel ready? Not the leap. The step.

This book will not teach you to silence the alarm. You can't, and you wouldn't want to — that same system will save your life one day. It will not make you fearless, or calm, or 'unstoppable.' It promises no rush of confidence at the moment you press send. What it offers is quieter and more durable: the ability to feel the alarm, name it for what it is, and move anyway — your hands still cold, your fear still present, the step taken regardless.

The fear is not a stop sign. It's a smoke detector that hasn't yet learned the difference between your kitchen and a wildfire. You can teach it. And until you do, you can act while it rings.

You were never broken. You were only well-defended. Let's bank that fire so it warms you instead of running you.

Book 1 of the Ember & Ash Publishing courage series.

books.author: Zane Vazquez

books.title: The Fraud Who Built a Company
books.subtitle: For the founder certain they're one mistake from being found out — meeting the hidden belief that you have to be brave for two
books.desc: Some part of you is sure that if anyone looked closely enough, they'd see you don't belong here. This is for the founder who overworks to cover a defect they can't name — who reads every brave act as a chance to finally be exposed. You'll learn where that belief came from, why courage feels so costly when something underneath says you're not enough, and how to act without first earning the right.
books.long_desc:
There's a version of the story you never tell. In it, you're not a founder — you're someone who has fooled a room full of smart people, and any day now the right question will expose you for good.

You know the feeling. The win that should land lands hollow, because you're already braced for the moment they find out. The opportunity you don't pursue, because pursuing it means being seen, and being seen means being caught. The exhausting hours you put in not to build something great but to cover the gap where you're certain something is missing in you. This is shame — not the feeling that you did something wrong, but the buried belief that something is wrong with you, and that courage is a privilege you have to earn before you're allowed any.

This book is for the founder who hides. The one who built a company and still feels like a fraud running it. The one for whom every brave act — the pitch, the price raise, the hard ask — doubles as an audition for exposure. You are not actually a fraud, and you are not uniquely broken. You are carrying an old, quiet conviction that you are not enough as you are, and you have been outworking it instead of meeting it.

Chapter by chapter, you'll come to recognize the hidden belief by name and trace where it learned to live in you. You'll feel the particular weight of having to be brave for two — to summon courage and first prove you deserve to have it. You'll understand why shame makes every visible act feel dangerous, and how the hiding costs you far more than the exposure ever would. And you'll meet the cliff-edge question that runs through this whole series: what is one thing you can do before you feel ready? Here it means: before you feel worthy. You don't have to earn the step.

This book will not give you a confidence makeover or a list of affirmations to repeat until you believe you're enough. It will not declare you healed of the feeling that you're a fraud. It promises no permanent arrival at self-worth. What it offers is gentler and truer: the recognition that the defect you've been hiding was never really there, and that you can act — visibly, bravely, imperfectly — without first passing a test you've been failing on purpose your whole life.

You were never the fraud. You were the one who cared so much about being found out that you forgot you were the real thing all along.

Come out of hiding by inches. The fire under the ash was always yours.

Book 3 of the Ember & Ash Publishing courage series.
books.angle: You're not afraid of the work. You're afraid they'll see who's doing it.
books.author: Zane Vazquez

books.title: One Thought, A Thousand Endings
books.subtitle: For the founder who plays out every catastrophe before deciding — quieting the spin so a single brave step can break the loop
books.desc: One worry becomes ten, then a hundred, each one a fully rendered disaster. This is for the founder whose mind spirals through every way it could go wrong until they're too dizzy to move. You'll learn how the loop feeds itself, why thinking harder makes it worse, and how one small action does what an hour of rumination never can.
books.long_desc:
It started as a single thought: what if this doesn't work?

An hour later you've lived through the failed launch, the refunds, the email you'll have to send, the look on your co-founder's face, the slow unraveling of everything you built. None of it has happened. All of it feels real. You are exhausted, and you still haven't done the one thing you sat down to do.

This is the spiral. One thought becomes a thousand, each branching into the next, every branch a more vivid catastrophe. It feels like preparation — like if you can just think it all the way through, you'll be safe. But the loop has no exit, because its real job isn't to solve anything. Its job is to keep you circling the edge so you never have to step off it.

This book is for the founder who ruminates. The one who mistakes spinning for strategizing, who reopens the same decision forty times a day, who lies awake war-gaming a conversation that will take four minutes in real life. You are not indecisive, and you are not weak. You have a fast, fertile mind that has learned to use its own brilliance against you — generating a thousand endings so you never have to risk the one real beginning.

Chapter by chapter, you'll watch the spiral up close and learn to recognize the moment it begins — the first thought that splits into two. You'll understand why thinking harder tightens the loop instead of loosening it, and why the body, not the mind, holds the exit. You'll meet the cliff-edge question that anchors this whole series: what is one thing you can do before you feel ready? Because a single small action does what an hour of rumination never can — it ends the rehearsal and starts the thing.

This book will not give you a thought-stopping trick or a five-step framework to 'master your mindset.' It won't promise a quiet mind or a clean inbox of worries. It will not optimize you. What it offers is the recognition that you cannot out-think a spiral, and the relief of finally stepping out of one — not by winning the argument in your head, but by setting it down and moving your feet.

The spinning isn't a sign you need to plan more. It's a sign you're ready, and afraid, and circling. You're allowed to step before the spiral resolves. It never will.

Let the loop turn without you. The step is still there, waiting, and it's smaller than the disaster you imagined.

Book 2 of the Ember & Ash Publishing courage series.

books.title: Their Embers Look Like Bonfires From Here
books.subtitle: For the founder certain every other room is full of people who truly belong
books.desc: You scroll the launch announcements, the funding posts, the conference photos where everyone looks like they were born knowing how to do this, and you feel the gap open up between them and you. This is the book for the founder who keeps weighing a hidden, fraudulent feeling against everyone who looks like they belong, and who never once comes out ahead.
books.long_desc:
You see them everywhere. The founder who raised twice what you did and seems unbothered by any of it. The peer on the panel speaking like certainty is her resting state. The feed full of people who look like they were issued the confidence you've spent years counterfeiting. And every time, the same quiet arithmetic runs underneath: they belong here, and you snuck in.

This book is for the entrepreneur whose imposter syndrome speaks the language of comparison — who can build something genuinely good and still lose a whole day measuring it against everyone who looks calm, finished, and sure. You're not arrogant and you're not fishing for reassurance. You've simply set your own raw, unedited insides on one side of the scale and everyone else's published highlight reel on the other, then called the result the truth about your worth.

You'll come to recognize how comparison feeds the fraud feeling instead of settling it — how every round you lose becomes more evidence that you don't belong, and how the scale was weighted against you before you ever stepped on it. You'll see the quiet sleight of hand: that you weigh your behind-the-scenes against their front-of-house, your private panic against their public ease. You'll notice, too, that the calm you envy is mostly a thing you can't see into — that the founder you're sure has it all figured out is, in her own dark office, late, measuring herself against someone too.

This is not a book about winning the comparison. It will not coach you to out-hustle the feed, to manifest a bigger raise, or to build the kind of success that finally makes you feel like you belong. That feeling doesn't live on the far side of the next milestone, and chasing it there is a good part of how you got this cold in the first place. Nothing in these pages will help you come out ahead of anyone. That, quietly, is the point.

What's here instead is warmer and slower: a way to step off the scale entirely. To feel that belonging was never something the others were issued and you missed at the door — it's just warmth, and you've been standing too far from the fire, too busy measuring, to let it reach your hands. You don't have to win to deserve to be here. You're already in the room. You built your own way in.

Book 2 of the Ember & Ash Publishing Imposter Syndrome series.
books.angle: You're reading their highlight reel against your raw footage. Of course you're losing.
books.author: Tara Oakes

books.title: They're Going to Find Out You Faked It
books.subtitle: For the founder who keeps overworking to cover a defect no one else can find — tending the quiet shame that the success was luck and the bill is coming due
books.desc: You closed the round, shipped the product, signed the client — and somewhere underneath the relief is a cold certainty that you got away with something, and that one day the right person will look closely and see the fraud you've been hiding. This is the book for the part of you that has been overworking to cover a defect no one else can find, because you're terrified the defect is you.
books.long_desc:
It happens right after the win. The deal closes, the launch lands, the board nods — and instead of warmth, a draft moves through your chest. They're going to find out. Not what you did, but who you are underneath it: someone who has been faking competence so convincingly that the exposure, when it comes, will be total.

This book is for the founder who carries shame the way you carry your phone — close, constant, barely noticed until it buzzes. You're not lazy. You're not failing. By every external measure you are succeeding, which is exactly the problem, because somewhere along the way you decided the success was luck and the bill is coming due. So you overwork. You over-prepare. You stay late in the dark office covering a defect you're sure lives in you, and the covering itself becomes proof that the defect is real.

You'll come to recognize shame as the cold thing banked at the center of all of it — the buried belief that something is wrong with you, not your spreadsheet, not your pitch, you. You'll see how it hides behind your competence and feeds on every achievement instead of being fed by it. You'll feel the difference between the failure you can name and the wrongness you can't, and understand at last why no win has ever once reached the cold place or warmed it for more than an evening.

This is not a confidence book. It will not teach you to fake it harder, to power-pose your way past the fear, or to turn the shame into one more thing to manage before morning. There is no exposure coming that you need to outrun, and nothing here pretends otherwise. What's here instead is slower and warmer: a way of sitting beside the cold place long enough to feel that it was never a defect at all. It was a fire someone let go out a long time ago, and you have been carrying the ash ever since, mistaking it for the truth about who you are.

You don't have to earn your way out of this. You don't have to deserve the warmth before you let yourself feel it. The work you built is real, and so is the part of you that went cold building it — and both can be true at once while you let one of them, finally, begin to thaw.

Book 1 of the Ember & Ash Publishing Imposter Syndrome series.

books.title: The Smoke Alarm That Never Sleeps
books.subtitle: For the founder whose body braces for a crisis that isn't coming — learning to tell a real fire from the alarm crying wolf
books.desc: Your inbox is quiet, the numbers are fine, and nobody is angry with you. Still, your chest is tight, your jaw is set, and your whole body is braced for a blow that never lands. This is the book for the founder whose alarm system has forgotten how to stand down. It won't teach you to fight the fire. It will teach you to notice there isn't one.
books.long_desc:
You check the dashboard one more time. Green. You re-read the email you already sent. Fine. The company isn't on fire — and yet your body has been bracing all day for something it can't name, a blow that never comes, a crisis that keeps not arriving.

This book is for the founder whose alarm went off years ago and never reset. The one who built something real by staying ready for everything, and now can't tell their nervous system that the danger has passed. You're not paranoid and you're not weak. You installed a smoke alarm sensitive enough to catch the first wisp of trouble — and it served you. The problem is it now screams at burnt toast. It mistakes a slow-paying client for ruin, a cool reply for catastrophe, a quiet Tuesday for the calm before something terrible.

Here you'll come to recognize the false alarm for what it is. The bracing in your shoulders before a call that goes fine. The 3am certainty that everything is collapsing, gone by daylight. The way your body floods with adrenaline over a Slack notification, then has nowhere to put it. You'll learn the difference between the fire that needs you and the alarm that's only crying wolf — not by arguing with the fear, but by letting your body finish the alarm it never got to complete.

This book will not promise you calm on command. It won't optimize your stress response or hand you a morning routine that makes the fear go away by 9am. There is no hack here, no productivity gain, nothing to grind toward. The relentless vigilance that exhausts you was trying to keep you safe, and you can't shame it into silence. You can only show it, gently and repeatedly, that the room is not burning.

What survives the false alarm isn't a harder, more armored you. It's the warmth that was always underneath — the founder who can feel their own pulse settle, who can sit in a quiet hour without reading it as a threat, who can finally let the fire bank low instead of blazing through the night.

You don't have to stay this braced. You were never the emergency. You were just the one who never got to hear the all-clear.

Book 1 of the Ember & Ash Publishing overthinking series.
books.angle: Your body keeps calling fire in an empty, quiet room.
books.author: Wren Frost

books.title: One Worry, Then a Thousand
books.subtitle: For the founder whose mind turns a single doubt into a midnight wildfire — and how to stop feeding the flames
books.desc: It begins with one small thing — a number that's slightly off, a reply that came back short. By midnight it's a wildfire: the deal, the runway, the team, and your whole worth as a founder, all of it ablaze in your head. This is the book for the mind that turns a single ember into an inferno. It won't help you think your way out. It will help you stop pouring on fuel.
books.long_desc:
It always starts small. One number slightly below projection. One client who 'just wants to talk.' One word in a reply that landed wrong. A single ember — and then your mind reaches for the kindling.

Within an hour it's everything. If this deal slips, the quarter slips, and if the quarter slips, the raise falls through, and if the raise falls through you have to let people go, and if you let people go you were never really cut out for this, and you're lying in the dark at 2am watching the whole forest of your life go up because of one cool email you can't even quote correctly anymore.

This book is for the founder whose worry doesn't stay one worry. The one whose mind takes a real, manageable concern and breeds it into a thousand catastrophes, each thought catching the next like dry brush in August. You're not irrational. You built something by following threads to their ends, by seeing five moves ahead — and that same gift, turned inward at midnight, becomes a fire that feeds itself.

Here you'll come to recognize the spiral by its shape, not its content. The way it always disguises itself as planning. The way every loop down feels like it's almost reaching an answer, and never does. The way the thinking generates its own heat — more thought, more dread, more thought — until you're not solving anything, you're just burning. You'll learn that the spiral is not asking to be finished. It's asking to be fed, and the only way out is to stop handing it fuel.

This book will not promise to make you stop thinking. It won't optimize your decision-making or hand you a framework that resolves the doubt by morning. There's no hack here, no five-step system, nothing to grind through. The spiral can't be out-thought, because thinking is the very thing it runs on. You don't escape a fire by adding logs to it.

What's left when the spiral burns down isn't a blank, fearless mind. It's the founder who can hold a single worry as a single worry — who can let one ember stay an ember, glowing and harmless, instead of the first spark of the end. The forest doesn't have to burn every night. You just have to stop being the one who lights it.

You are allowed to leave a thought unfinished. Not every doubt is a doorway you have to walk all the way through.

Book 2 of the Ember & Ash Publishing overthinking series.
books.angle: It started with one ember. By 2am the whole forest was burning.
books.author: Wren Frost

books.title: The Mind That Won't Bank for the Night
books.subtitle: For the founder whose attention never stops tending the fire — and the slow way back to letting it rest
books.desc: Some part of you is always tending the fire — checking the numbers, the room, the tone of every message, certain that if you look away for one second everything goes dark or spreads. This is the book for the founder whose mind never banks for the night. It won't ask you to drop your guard all at once. It will help you finally let that tired part of you warm its own hands.
books.long_desc:
You are watching right now. Even reading this, some part of you is scanning — the half-thought about tomorrow's standup, the tone of that last message replaying, the low background hum that says don't ease off yet, not yet, not while there's still one more thing to keep an eye on.

This is the book for the founder whose mind never banks for the night. The one who learned, somewhere back in the early days when everything depended on you catching it first, to keep one part of yourself tending the fire at all hours. You check the dashboard before you notice your own hunger. You read the room before you read how you feel. You go to bed with one eye open because the company has felt, for years, like a coal you alone have to keep breath on through the dark — sure that the moment you look away, it goes out, or it spreads.

Here you'll come to recognize that tending in its hundred disguises. The reflexive refresh. The way you replay a conversation hunting for the thing you missed. The inability to be fully in a dinner, a walk, a weekend, because some part of you stayed back at the hearth. The exhaustion that isn't about how much you've done but about how long you've been keeping watch — bracing against trouble that, night after night, never actually comes. You'll learn that this vigilant part of you is not the enemy. It's a loyal, tired thing that was never told it could rest, still crouched over a fire that has long since banked itself low and safe.

This book will not promise to switch the watchfulness off. It won't optimize your focus or teach you to monitor more efficiently. There's no hack here, no system for scanning smarter, nothing to grind toward. You can't make this part of you settle by shouting at it — it only crouches lower. You can only come close to it, slowly and with respect, and begin the long work of telling it the night is quiet, the coals will hold, and it's allowed to warm its own hands for a while.

What comes back when that tired part of you finally lowers its eyes isn't carelessness. It's presence. The founder who can sit in a room without reading it for exits. Who can hear good news without immediately scanning for the catch. Who can feel first light on their hands at dawn and, for once, not be looking past it toward the next thing that might go wrong.

The long night you've been keeping watch through has been over for a while. The fire is banked. You're allowed to lie down beside it and close both eyes.

Book 3 of the Ember & Ash Publishing overthinking series.
books.angle: You've kept the fire all night for years. It banked itself long ago.

books.title: Measuring Your Coals Against Their Flames
books.subtitle: For the founder weighing a quiet inner banking against everyone who looks ablaze
books.desc: Your feed is a wall of founders who raised, scaled, and look completely fine — and somewhere in the scroll your own worth quietly evaporates. This is a book for the founder who's running a brutal scoreboard no one else can see. It won't help you win the comparison. That's the whole point.
books.long_desc:
It happens in the gaps. Between calls, in the line for coffee, in the ninety seconds before sleep — your thumb finds the feed and there they all are. The founder who just announced the round. The one whose product looks finished and easy. The one your age, or younger, who seems to have figured out the thing you're still bleeding over. And without deciding to, you do the math: where they are, where you are, how far behind that makes you. The number always comes back the same. Short.

This book is for the founder running that scoreboard. The one who can have a good week and lose it the moment someone else has a better one. The one who reads a competitor's launch as a verdict on their own worth, who can't celebrate a win because someone is always a lap ahead, who has quietly decided that everyone else got an instruction manual and you got the version with pages missing.

You'll come to recognize the rigged game underneath it. How you compare the inside of your business — the doubt, the cash-flow nights, the half-built thing held together by your hands — to the outside of theirs, the announcement, the highlight, the calm face on the panel. How comparison dresses up as ambition but spends like a tax, taking a little of your worth every time you scroll. How the scoreboard never closes, never declares you enough, because being enough was never a score you could win.

This book will not help you come out ahead. It will not give you a way to finally beat the comparison, turn your envy into fuel, or make the feed into motivation. There's no reframe here that makes you the founder who's winning. That's not a flaw in the plan; that's the plan. Because the only way out of a rigged game is to stop agreeing to keep score.

You were never behind. You've just been holding the same bright, tireless attention that built this company up to the light beside other people's — measuring your unfinished days against lives you'll never see the full ledger of. You're allowed to set the scoreboard down. You're allowed to have a worth that doesn't move when someone else raises, ships, or shines.

The ember in you doesn't burn brighter or dimmer based on the fire across the room. It was never in the running. Let it warm your own cold hands for once.

Book 2 of the Ember & Ash Publishing self-worth series.

books.title: The Crack You're Sure the Light Shows Through
books.subtitle: For the founder convinced there's a flaw in them the next failure will expose
books.desc: You're convinced there's a flaw at the center of you, and you've built a business loud enough to keep anyone from seeing it. This is a book for the founder who works not to win but to stay ahead of being found out. It won't tell you to believe in yourself harder. It will sit with the part of you that never thought you were enough, and warm it.
books.long_desc:
There's a feeling you carry into every meeting, every pitch, every 1:1, and you've never said it out loud because saying it would make it true. The feeling is that something is wrong with you. Not your strategy. You. And the whole enterprise you've built — the hours, the standards no one asked for, the smile you put on when you're hollowed out — exists to keep that flaw covered, to stay one step ahead of the day someone looks closely and sees what you're sure is there.

This book is for that founder. The one who reads their own wins as luck and their own losses as proof. The one who can't accept the compliment because the compliment was given to the version of you that's still hiding something. The one who is exhausted not by the work but by the covering — the second job, the unpaid one, of making sure no one ever catches the defect at the center of you.

You'll come to recognize the shape of it. How shame doesn't shout; it whispers, in your own voice, that you got away with it again. How it turns a missed number into a character verdict. How it makes you overwork, over-deliver, over-apologize, all to outrun a sentence that was handed to you long before this company existed. You'll see how often the thing you're so sure is broken in you is the very place you're most tender, most human, most worth tending.

This book will not hand you back to yourself fixed. There is no protocol here, no morning routine that finally makes you enough, no reframe that optimizes the shame away. It will not tell you the flaw isn't real and it will not tell you to push through it. It will do something quieter and harder: it will sit beside the part of you that never believed it belonged, and it will not flinch.

You don't have to earn your way out of this. You don't have to perform your worth for one more quarter, or build one more thing to prove the bad thing wrong. You're allowed to be a person whose value was never on the table to begin with — even on the days the business is failing, even on the days you are sure you are too.

Let the covering rest. There's an ember under all that ash, and it was never the flaw. It was you.

books.title: Your Heart Is Loud and Nothing Is Wrong
books.subtitle: For the teen whose body sounds the alarm before they've said a single word — when raising your hand feels like stepping off a cliff
books.desc: Your hand is halfway up and your whole body slams the brakes — heart pounding, ears hot, a flood of certainty that something terrible is about to happen. Nothing is actually about to happen. This book is for the teenager whose alarm system fires at ordinary moments and treats a question, a presentation, a hello like a threat. It won't tell you to push through. It'll show you what's burning and how to stand near the heat without running.
books.long_desc:
It's a normal Tuesday. The teacher asks a question you actually know the answer to. And before you can decide anything, your body has already decided for you — heart going, hands cold, a hot wave up your neck and a voice in your chest that says don't, not this, something is wrong. Nothing is wrong. The room is safe. But try telling that to the part of you that's already braced like you're about to fall.

This book is for the teenager who lives with a smoke alarm that goes off when there's no fire. You're not dramatic and you're not weak. You have a fast, loud warning system that can't tell the difference between real danger and the ordinary scary stuff of being fifteen — being seen, being called on, walking into a room where you don't know if you belong. It sounds the same alarm for all of it. So you've learned to stay small, stay quiet, keep your hand down, and call it not caring when really it's just easier than the flood.

Chapter by chapter, you'll come to recognize the false alarm for what it is. You'll feel the way it grabs you before a single thought arrives, and learn that the racing heart is not a prophecy — it's just heat, and heat passes. You'll see how avoiding the scary thing teaches the alarm that it was right to panic, and how doing one small brave thing while you're still shaking teaches it something new. There's a question this whole book keeps coming back to, and it's not 'how do I make the fear stop.' It's: what is one thing you can do before you feel ready?

This book will not make you fearless. It won't hand you confidence or a morning routine or a hack to silence the alarm forever. The alarm is part of how you're built, and the goal was never to rip the wiring out. The goal is to stop believing the alarm every time it screams. To feel your heart go loud, notice that nothing is actually on fire, and take the step anyway — small, scared, real.

You don't have to wait until you feel safe to be brave. You were never going to feel safe first. You get to be brave with a loud heart. That's the only kind of brave there is.

books.title: The Part of You That You Hide
books.subtitle: For the teen who's sure something is wrong with them — when bravery feels like a thing other people are allowed and you are not
books.desc: Somewhere underneath everything, you carry a quiet certainty that there's something wrong with you — and most of what you do is built to keep anyone from finding out. This book is for the teenager who hides the real thing and overworks to cover it. It won't tell you to love yourself or fake it till you make it. It'll sit with the part of you that you've been hiding, and tell you the truth: you were never the defect you think you are.
books.long_desc:
You can't always name it, but you can feel it — a low, steady certainty that something about you is wrong. Not a thing you did. You. Bravery, you've decided, is for the people who don't have whatever it is you have. They get to step forward. You get to hide.

This book is for the teenager who carries shame like a second backbone. The buried belief that you're somehow too much or not enough, defective in a way everyone else got spared — and the enormous effort it takes to keep that hidden. You're not actually broken. But you've been treating yourself like contraband for so long that the hiding feels like just who you are. It isn't. It's armor you put on young, for reasons that made sense, and it's gotten very heavy.

Chapter by chapter, you'll come to recognize the shame underneath the strategies. You'll see how the hiding costs you the exact things you most want — to be known, to try, to take up space — and how the belief that something's wrong with you isn't a fact you discovered but a story you absorbed. You'll learn that courage here isn't a big brave performance; it's the small, terrifying act of letting one true thing show. There's a question this book keeps returning to, and it isn't 'how do I fix what's wrong with me.' It's: what is one thing you can do before you feel ready — before you feel worthy of being seen?

This book will not promise to make you finally good enough. There's no glow-up, no self-love checklist, no version of you that earns its way out of shame by achieving hard enough. That road has no end and you've been running it long enough to know. What it offers instead is this: the possibility that there was never anything to cover in the first place. That the part of you you've been hiding is just a part of you — scorched, maybe, but not ruined. The kind of thing that grows back green.

You don't have to become someone else to be allowed in the room. You only have to stop believing you don't belong there. Let one true thing show. Start there.

It's: what is one thing you can do before you feel ready — before the spiral gives you permission?

This book will not promise to quiet your mind for good. It won't hand you a thought-stopping trick or a clever reframe that makes the loop dissolve every time. Your mind is going to spin again. The point isn't to never spiral — it's to stop trusting the spiral as if it were telling you the truth. To notice the pull, feel it try to take you down, and step off the wheel while it's still turning.

What's left when you stop is smaller and warmer than winning. Your own work, done at your own pace. A morning that doesn't begin by counting how far back you've fallen. The first-light understanding that you were never behind at all — you were only watching everyone else instead of the doorway right in front of you, the one with your name on it.

By the time you sit down to work, your hands are cold and your heart is going like you're being chased. But nothing is chasing you. It's a Tuesday. It was only ever a Tuesday.

This book is for you — the student whose alarm system has a hair trigger. You grew up measured and ranked and watched, and somewhere along the way your body decided that an ordinary day is something to survive. You're not broken and you're not dramatic. You've just got a smoke detector that goes off when you make toast. That's not a character flaw. That's a fire that nobody taught you to read.

This is not a method to make you fearless. It will not optimize your mornings or hack your cortisol or hand you a routine to win the day. There is no winning the day. There is only learning, slowly, that warmth is allowed to return to cold hands, and that an ordinary Tuesday can be met instead of survived.

Nothing here will switch the alarm off for good. That's not the point. The point is that you stop treating every spark like a wildfire — that you learn to put a hand on your own chest and say, gently, not this time. The fire in you was never the enemy. It only needed banking, not dousing.

You are allowed to be safe before you've earned it. You are allowed to set the alarm down.

You've got a smoke detector wired straight into your nervous system, and it has learned to go off at a slammed door, an unanswered text, a tone you imagined in a one-word reply. It's not broken. This is not a book that will teach you to stop feeling afraid. It won't optimize your nervous system or hand you a morning routine that makes the dread go quiet forever. It will not promise you a fix, because you are not broken. Your alarm loves you. It has been trying to keep you safe in a world that taught you everything was urgent. That's not a defect to delete. It's a warmth to tend.

What you'll find instead is recognition — the kind that loosens the grip in your chest a half-inch at a time. You'll learn to put a hand on the alarm and say, gently, not this time. To let the bracing soften before you've solved anything. To trust that you can feel the heat and still know there's no fire.

You are allowed to feel the alarm and not obey it. Start there.

books.long_desc:
Here is the thing you've never said out loud: you don't actually believe you earned any of it. The acceptance, the grades, the kind words from the professor who clearly hasn't figured you out yet. Underneath all of it sits a quiet, total certainty that there is something wrong with you — something defective at the core — and that everything you do is a costume you wear so that no one will ever see it.

This is for you — the student who works twice as hard not to succeed but to cover. The one who deflects every compliment, who is sure praise is just evidence you've fooled them again, who feels not the fear of failing but the deeper dread of being found out. Shame is different from doubt. Doubt says you might not be good enough. Shame says you are not enough, that the flaw is you, and that exposure is only a matter of time. It is the most isolating feeling there is, because its whole job is to keep you hiding.

You'll come to recognize shame's particular voice — how it speaks in absolutes, how it turns 'I made a mistake' into 'I am a mistake,' how it makes you overwork not toward a goal but away from a defect that was never really there. You'll see how it lives in the body: the heat in your face, the urge to disappear, the flinch when someone looks at you too closely. You'll learn the difference between hiding the flaw and tending the person, and why bringing it into the light is the one thing shame can't survive.

This book will not tell you to love yourself. It won't hand you affirmations to recite into a mirror, and it won't pretend that a buried sense of unworthiness dissolves because someone on a page told you you're worthy. There's no fix here for the feeling, no optimization that makes you finally enough. What there is, instead, is company — a warm voice in the room with the part of you that has been certain it had to stay hidden, and a slow, patient unmaking of the belief that you are the defect.

You were never the thing that needed covering. The fire was always there under the ash. You can stop guarding it so hard now.

books.title: I Replayed That Seminar Until 4am
books.subtitle: For the student who can't stop rerunning the thing they said in class — when one cringe becomes a thousand and the loop feeds itself
books.desc: You said one slightly-off thing in the discussion eight hours ago and your brain has been playing it on a loop ever since, each replay adding new evidence that everyone now knows you're a fraud. This is for the student whose mind turns a single moment into a thousand. It won't promise to stop the thoughts — it will help you stop feeding the fire that keeps them burning.
books.long_desc:
You said one thing in the seminar. It wasn't even wrong, really — just a little off, a little too eager, a half-second too long. That was at two in the afternoon. It is now the middle of the night and your mind has played it back roughly four hundred times, and each replay grows a new branch: what they must have thought, what it proves about you, what it means for every room you'll ever walk into after this.

This is for you — the student whose mind is a courtroom that never adjourns. The one who rereads sent messages for hidden disasters, who narrates their own mistakes back to themselves on a loop, who lies in bed building an entire case for why they don't belong out of one small ambiguous moment. The spiral isn't thinking. It's the same thought wearing a thousand costumes, pulling you down and down, and the worst part is that it feels productive — like if you just replay it enough times you'll finally solve it. You won't. That's not what it's for.

You'll come to recognize the difference between a thought and the loop a thought gets caught in. You'll see how imposter syndrome is the perfect fuel for rumination: every mistake becomes proof of the fraud you're sure you are, and proof demands review, and review demands more proof. You'll learn how the spiral feeds itself, how it dresses up as problem-solving, and what it actually takes to step off the wheel — not by arguing with the thoughts, which only adds logs to the fire, but by stopping the feeding.

This book will not promise to make your mind go quiet. It won't hand you a thought-stopping trick that deletes the loop on command, and it won't pretend you can think your way out of a process that runs on thinking. There's no fixing a mind that does this; there's only learning to stop pouring fuel on it. What it offers is the thing the spiral keeps you from believing is possible: that you can let the thought arrive, and not chase it, and watch the fire bank itself down to warmth.

The loop ends when you stop feeding it. Not before, and not by force — just by setting down the wood.

Book 3 of the Ember & Ash Publishing Imposter Syndrome series.

books.title: Watching Yourself Get the Degree from Somewhere Far Away
books.subtitle: For the student who feels like a spectator at their own life — numb, behind glass, narrating the day instead of living it
books.desc: You're in the room, doing the work, saying the right things, and the whole time it feels like you're watching someone else do it from behind glass. This is for the student who has gone numb and far away, monitoring their own life instead of living it. It won't snap you back to feeling on command — it will gently walk you toward the part of you that's been waiting outside the window.
books.long_desc:
You're in the lecture and you're also somewhere else. Your hand is taking notes, your face is doing the right things, and the real you is a few feet back and slightly above, watching a stranger who looks like you go through the motions of being a student. People say it must be exciting. You smile and agree and feel almost nothing, like there's glass between you and your own life, and you can't remember when it went up.

This is for you — the student who has gone numb and far away. The one who narrates the day instead of living it, who watches themselves get the grade or give the presentation with a flat, distant detachment, who scans every room for what might go wrong and never quite arrives inside the moment. This watcher in you isn't lazy or cold. It's a guard that went on duty when being fully present started to feel too exposed, and forgot how to clock off. It keeps you safe by keeping you outside — monitoring threats that never come, at the cost of every good thing that does.

You'll come to recognize the watcher's particular flavor of survival — the numbness that isn't peace, the hypervigilance that never rests, the strange grief of achieving the thing you wanted and feeling nothing as it happens. You'll see how imposter syndrome recruits this part of you: if you never fully show up, you reason, you can never fully be found out. You'll learn the difference between watching your life and living it, and why the way back isn't to force feeling but to make it safe enough to land.

This book will not snap you back into your body on command. It won't promise a technique that floods the numbness with feeling, or tell you to just be present as if presence were a switch you've been too lazy to flip. There's no optimizing your way through dissociation, no fixing a part of you that has been working overtime to protect you. What there is, instead, is patience — a slow, warm walk toward the part of you standing outside the window, and an invitation, with no pressure attached, to come back in.

The watcher can stand down. Not because you ordered it to, but because, a little at a time, it's becoming safe enough to feel your own life happening to you again.

Book 7 of the Ember & Ash Publishing Imposter Syndrome series.

books.title: They All Seem to Carry It Lighter
books.subtitle: For the student sure their classmates think half as hard and worry half as much
books.desc: Everyone else seems to have figured something out that nobody told you. This is for the overthinker who measures their churning mind against every classmate who looks calm, sorted, ahead — and always loses. It won't help you catch up. That's the point.
books.long_desc:
Open the phone and there they are: someone's internship, someone's relationship, someone's effortless certainty about exactly what they're doing with their life. You're up at midnight with a mind that won't quiet, and everyone on the screen looks calm, finished, sure. It seems like they all got a manual you never received — and so the thinking turns on the only target left: you, lit by a screen, certain you're the one who's behind.

This book is for the student who measures themselves against everyone who looks fine. You take your loudest, most anxious inside and hold it up against their quietest, most curated outside, and call the gap a verdict. The classmate who answers so easily. The friend who seems to have it all sorted. The version of your peers that exists only in highlight reels and in your head at 1 a.m. You have turned a sharp, restless mind toward a contest you can only lose, and you run it again and again, looking for the proof that you're behind.

Here you'll come to recognize comparison as the engine driving so much of the overthinking. How the mind builds a leaderboard nobody else is standing on. How 'everyone else has it handled' is a story made of glimpses, and how your overthinking treats it as data. You'll learn what it feels like to catch yourself mid-measurement and set the ruler down. You'll see that the people who look calm are mostly just people you can't see clearly. And you'll find that your worth was never a ranking — that the warmth of your own life can't be felt while you're busy grading it against someone else's.

Nothing here will help you win the comparison. That's the point. This book won't give you a glow-up plan or a way to finally pull ahead. It won't promise that if you measure yourself hard enough, you'll be the one others envy. It refuses to enter you in the race at all. What it offers instead is the relief of stepping off the leaderboard — of being a person, in a life, rather than a rank in a feed.

You are not behind. There was never a line, and there was never a manual. You are allowed to close the comparison and warm your hands at the small, real fire of your own life.

Book 6 of the Ember & Ash Publishing overthinking series.

books.title: Nothing Is Wrong and You Still Can't Breathe
books.subtitle: For the student whose body sounds the alarm at 2 a.m. over a text, a grade, a tone you can't name — bracing for danger that never comes
books.desc: You are safe and your chest does not believe it. This is for the overthinker whose body keeps firing the alarm over things that haven't happened and probably won't — the unread text, the look in a seminar, the deadline two weeks out. It will not talk you out of the feeling. It will sit with you until the warmth comes back.
books.long_desc:
It comes on without warning. You are lying in bed, or halfway across the quad, or staring at a half-typed message, and suddenly your chest is tight and your hands are cold and some old part of you is absolutely certain that something is wrong. You scan for the fire. You can't find it. And so the mind, helpful as ever, decides the fire must be you.

This book is for the student whose alarm never quite quiets. You are not in danger and your body has not gotten the message. A professor's flat reply, a friend who left you on read, a grade you haven't seen yet — and the same surge arrives, the same bracing, as if the worst were already here. You have spent so long living at the edge of an emergency that calm itself can feel suspicious, like the quiet before something.

Here you'll come to recognize the false alarm for what it is. How the body confuses a hard feeling with a real threat. How overthinking is the mind trying to find a danger big enough to justify the dread — and how the search itself keeps the alarm ringing. You'll learn what it feels like when the surge crests and breaks on its own, without you fixing anything, without you figuring it out. You'll see that the absence of catastrophe is not luck you have to earn each day. It is just the truth your nervous system hasn't caught up to yet.

This book will not promise you a calm you can summon on command. It won't hand you a breathing technique and call you fixed. It won't pretend the alarm will never sound again — it will, because you are wired to care, and that wiring is not a defect. What it offers instead is recognition: a warm hand on a cold shoulder, a voice in the next room saying you're alright, you're alright, even when your body swears you aren't.

There is nothing to brace for tonight. You are allowed to let the alarm ring out and discover that nothing was coming. You are allowed to feel the warmth return to your hands. You are allowed, finally, to let the alarm go quiet.

Book 1 of the Ember & Ash Publishing overthinking series.

books.title: The Version of Me That Had It Figured Out
books.subtitle: For the student mourning a future that didn't arrive — when overthinking is really grief for what was lost, or what must finally be set down
books.desc: Sometimes the overthinking is grief wearing a thinking face. This is for the student looping over a version of life that didn't happen — the plan that fell through, the person you thought you'd be, the certainty you've lost. It won't rush you to acceptance. It will let you set it down when you're ready, and not before.
books.long_desc:
There's a version of your life you can't stop returning to. The plan that made sense before everything shifted. The friendship that quietly ended. The certainty you had at eighteen about who you'd become, which the last few years have slowly taken apart. You tell yourself you're overthinking it, that you should be over it by now — but the mind keeps going back, circling the same loss, because some part of you was never given permission to grieve it.

This book is for the student whose overthinking is really mourning in disguise. Not every loop is anxiety. Some of it is grief — for a future that didn't arrive, for the person you were before a hard year, for a version of yourself who felt sure. You replay it not because you're stuck but because letting go feels like agreeing it's gone, and you weren't ready to agree. So you keep it alive in the only place it still lives: in the thinking, turned over and over, kept warm.

Here you'll come to recognize grief underneath the rumination. How the mind clings to what's lost by thinking about it, mistaking the loop for loyalty. How some overthinking is the heart refusing to set something down before it's been properly held. You'll learn the difference between the thinking that keeps a wound open and the slow, real work of letting go — which is not forgetting, and not getting over it, but loosening your grip one finger at a time. You'll find that what must finally be released doesn't have to be released today. And that grieving a future is allowed, even when no one died, even when you can't quite explain to anyone what you lost.

This book will not rush you to acceptance or tell you it was all for the best. It won't promise closure or a five-stage path out the other side. It refuses to treat your grief as a problem to think your way past. What it offers is room — room to mourn what didn't happen, to honor the self you're no longer, and to let go on your own slow clock, when the warmth is ready to return to the cold place.

What was lost was real, and so is what's still growing back. You do not have to put it down before you're ready. You are allowed to grieve, and to keep the green that comes after the burn.

Book 5 of the Ember & Ash Publishing overthinking series.

books.title: Everything Is Due and You Cannot Move
books.subtitle: For the student drowning in tabs, deadlines, and a to-do list too long to begin — when the flood arrives all at once and there's no room left to think
books.desc: Too much is arriving and your mind has run out of room. This is for the overthinker frozen at the start of an impossible week — every tab open, every deadline stacked, the list so long you can't begin any of it. It won't give you a system. It will help you find the small clearing where you can breathe.
books.long_desc:
You sit down to start and there is nowhere to start. The reading, the email you've drafted four times, the deadline you moved and then moved again, the laundry, the call home, the thing you said you'd do for a friend — it all arrives in the same second, in the same crowded breath, and your mind, asked to hold it, simply floods. Not lazy. Not behind. Just full past the line where thinking is even possible.

This book is for the student whose capacity got exceeded somewhere around Tuesday. You are not failing to manage your time. You have too much arriving and nowhere to set it down. Overthinking, for you, isn't a single loop — it's everything at once, a hundred half-thoughts shouting over each other until the noise itself becomes the thing you can't get past. You open the laptop and freeze. You scroll instead, not because you don't care but because caring this much, about this much, has nowhere to go.

Here you'll come to recognize the flood for what it is — capacity reached, not character revealed. You'll see how the overwhelmed mind treats every task as equally urgent and equally on fire, so that choosing where to begin feels impossible because nothing will give you permission to put the rest down. You'll learn what it feels like to find one small clearing in the smoke — not a method, not a system, just a single warm place to stand while the rest waits. You'll discover that the flood crests. It always crests. And that you do not have to hold the whole year in your two hands at 1 a.m.

This book will not tidy your week into five steps or hand you a planner that promises the flood won't come. It won't pretend that if you just plan harder, none of this would happen. It refuses to treat your overwhelm as a scheduling error. What it offers is the relief of being seen at capacity, and the quiet truth that you are allowed to do one thing, or no things, and still be someone who is doing their best.

The flood will recede. You do not have to fight the whole tide tonight. You are allowed to set it all down, just here, just for now, and feel the room come back.

Book 2 of the Ember & Ash Publishing overthinking series.

books.title: If They Knew How Much I Think About This
books.subtitle: For the student certain everyone else has it together — when overthinking hides a quieter belief that something is wrong with you
books.desc: Underneath all the thinking is a quieter sentence: something is wrong with me. This is for the overthinker who overworks every word to cover a flaw they're sure everyone would see. It won't tell you you're fine and move on. It will gently question whether the defect you've been hiding was ever there.
books.long_desc:
You don't tell people how much you think. How long you spend on a two-line text. How many times you replayed the thing you said before deciding it proved something about you. Because under the overthinking there's a quieter sentence, one you've never said out loud: that there is something wrong with you, specifically, and the thinking is the cost of keeping it hidden.

This book is for the student who overworks every interaction to cover a flaw they're certain everyone else would see. You don't think you have a problem — you think you are one. The endless drafting, the apologizing, the rehearsing, the watching their face for the second they figure you out: it isn't vanity and it isn't weakness. It's the work of someone who decided, a long time ago, that they were defective, and that constant effort was the rent on being allowed to stay.

Here you'll come to recognize shame as the ember underneath so much of the noise. How overthinking becomes a way to manage a self you believe is faulty — patch the gaps, predict the judgment, perform the version that might pass. How the certainty that you're broken feels less like a feeling and more like a fact, so old you've stopped questioning it. You'll learn what it's like to hear that sentence in your head and not automatically believe it. You'll begin to see the difference between making a mistake and being one — a difference shame has spent years blurring. And you'll find that the thing you've been hiding and overworking to cover was never the defect you took it for. It was just a tender place that never got tended.

This book will not tell you you're fine and hurry past it. It won't hand you affirmations to repeat into the mirror, or promise that enough self-love will burn the shame off for good. It refuses to treat your shame as a confidence problem with a confidence solution. What it offers is something slower and warmer: the recognition that you were never the flaw in the room, and permission to stop paying rent on a self that was never broken.

You do not have to earn your place here by thinking harder. You are allowed to be seen exactly as you are, ember and ash and all, and stay.

books.title: The Worry That Won't Burn Out
books.subtitle: For the student lying awake while one thought keeps reigniting the next
books.desc: One thought walked in and brought a thousand with it. This is for the overthinker pulled down by the spiral — the worry that splits and splits, the message read back until the words blur, the 3 a.m. rumination that feeds on itself. It won't help you win the argument in your head. It will help you step off the wheel.
books.long_desc:
It always starts with something small. A word someone used. A reply that came too fast or too slow. A thing you said in a seminar that nobody remembers but you. And then the one thought splits, and the two thoughts split, and within minutes you are somewhere far down, building whole futures out of a single glance, reading the same message back until the letters stop meaning anything, certain that if you just think about it long enough you'll find the bottom and finally be safe.

This book is for the student who knows the spiral by heart. Not occasional worry — the practiced descent, the loop that feeds on itself, the way one anxious thought becomes proof you should be anxious about everything else. You lie awake re-running a conversation, rewriting your lines, defending yourself to a judge who isn't in the room. You know, somewhere, that the thinking isn't getting you anywhere. You do it anyway, because stopping feels like leaving something dangerous unwatched.

Here you'll come to recognize the spiral as a shape, not a truth. How rumination disguises itself as problem-solving so that quitting feels irresponsible. How the mind keeps offering one more lap, one more angle, promising that this is the thought that finally settles it — and how that promise is the spiral's whole trick. You'll learn what it feels like to notice you've been pulled in without hating yourself for it, and to step off the wheel mid-turn, not because you solved it but because you finally let it be unsolved. You'll find that thoughts you stop feeding grow quiet on their own, the way a fire left unbanked goes gently to ash.

This book will not win the argument running in your head, because it can't be won — that's the point. It won't give you a thought-stopping trick that makes the spiral never come back. It won't shame you for the hours already lost down there. What it offers is a way to recognize the descent early and the permission to climb out unfinished, with the question still open, and survive it.

You do not have to think your way to the bottom of this tonight. There is no bottom; there is only the next quiet thing. You are allowed to step off the wheel and let the thought go cold.

Book 3 of the Ember & Ash Publishing overthinking series.

books.title: The Watchfulness That Outlasts the Lecture Hall
books.subtitle: For the student whose scanning mind keeps running long after class lets out
books.desc: There's a part of you that never rests — scanning faces, tracking tones, watching for the thing that's about to go wrong. This is for the overthinker whose vigilance never lets up. It won't tell that part of you to quit. It will show it, gently, that the danger it's bracing against isn't coming.
books.long_desc:
Even now, some part of you is watching. Reading the room. Clocking the slight change in a friend's tone, the pause before someone replied, the expression that flickered and vanished. You walk into a seminar already scanning for who's annoyed, who's ahead, what might go wrong. You leave a conversation and immediately replay it. The part of you that keeps watch never rests — it has been awake for years, and it's exhausted, and it cannot imagine being allowed to put the watching down.

This book is for the student whose mind never lets up. The hypervigilance that feels like care, or competence, or just who you are by now. You monitor everything because somewhere you learned that the unwatched moment is the dangerous one — that if you stop scanning, you'll miss the threat, the slight, the disaster you could have seen coming. So the overthinking never sleeps. It only changes its shape. And the strange, lonely cost is that you are never quite all the way in your own life, because part of you is always at the window, looking out for what's coming.

Here you'll come to recognize that watchful part for what it is: something that started keeping vigil in a harder season and was never told the danger had passed. How vigilance disguises itself as responsibility. How scanning for threats that never arrive keeps your whole body braced, certain the calm is just a gap before the next thing. You'll learn what it feels like to thank that part of you instead of fighting it — and to let it sit down by the fire, just for a while, and discover the room was safe the whole time. You'll find that you can pay attention to your life without keeping watch over it.

This book will not order that part of you to quit or shame you for being 'too much.' It won't promise a hypervigilance off-switch or a single technique that ends the scanning forever. It refuses to treat the part of you that's kept you safe as an enemy. What it offers instead is the slow, warm permission for the watching to finally rest — to learn that the threat it's been bracing against is not, after all, on its way.

The watch can end. Not because you forced it, but because nothing is coming. You are allowed to come in from the window, warm your hands, and be all the way home.

Book 7 of the Ember & Ash Publishing overthinking series.
